relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The final battle drives Israel's soldiers into flight and death on Gilboa.
Philistine forces overtake the royal family and kill Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchi-shua.
Saul is wounded and terrified under concentrated archery.
Fearing torture and public abuse, Saul asks his armor-bearer to kill him; when the terrified man refuses, Saul falls on his own sword.
The armor-bearer then dies by his own sword, joining rather than surviving the king.
Saul, three sons, the armor-bearer, and his immediate force die in one day.
Civilians and remaining communities flee abandoned towns, which Philistines occupy.
The next day the victors search and strip the bodies.
They behead Saul, remove his weapons, and circulate the victory through religious and public networks.
His armor becomes temple display and his body is exposed on Beth-shan's wall.
Jabesh-gilead remembers what Saul once did for the city and hears of the desecration.
Men travel through the night to recover the bodies and burn them at Jabesh.
They bury the bones beneath a tree and fast seven days, ending the book in communal grief rather than triumph.

contemplative_note: The book ends not with royal glory but with bodies, displaced cities, a night rescue, burial, and fasting. Jabesh-gilead restores dignity to the dead without erasing the damage of Saul's reign. Lament is the final word.
